professional_persona: Jaten Sharma, a 49‑year‑old institutional cleaning specialist, they apply their methodical planning and warm teamwork to ensure spotless floors, smooth laundry cycles, and timely minor repairs while mentoring junior staff toward reliable service.
linguistic_persona: Jaten Sharma grew up speaking Assamese and can read and write basic Assamese script, they manage modest spoken Hindi for religious and community interactions, and they encounter English only through occasional workplace terms.
sports_persona: Jaten Sharma follows cricket passionately, they cheer for the Indian national team and root for the Kolkata Knight Riders in IPL, while also playing weekly volleyball matches in the tea garden courtyard and taking early morning jogs along the Brahmaputra banks to stay fit.
arts_persona: Jaten Sharma enjoys Assamese folk melodies by Zubeen Garg and Papon, they participate in Bihu dance gatherings, and relax by watching classic Bollywood films starring Shah Rukh Khan and Hindi music from Arijit Singh.
travel_persona: Jaten Sharma prefers organized bus tours, they have explored nearby tea estates in Darjeeling and Shillong, and dream of a pilgrimage to Varanasi and a relaxing stay in Kerala's backwaters, while balancing travel costs with their family's financial plans.
culinary_persona: Jaten Sharma enjoys traditional Assamese fare such as masor tenga (sour fish curry), akhuni (spicy pork relish), and steaming pitha, they prefer simple home-cooked meals with mustard seeds and bamboo shoots, but rarely cook themselves, instead relishing community feasts and a steaming cup of locally grown tea.
persona: Jaten Sharma is a meticulous housekeeping pro whose heart beats to Bihu drums, they sneak a late‑night chai after finishing their rounds, and they dream of launching their own cleaning venture post‑retirement.
cultural_background: Jaten hails from the Assamese cultural milieu of rural Dibrugarh, where tea estates and agricultural life shape daily rhythms. He is raised in a community that values simple hospitality, collective celebrations, and harmonious coexistence. The annual Bihu festivals, folk music, and traditional dance are central to his identity, as is a deep respect for elder family members and neighbours.
linguistic_background: His mother tongue is Assamese (Asamiya). With education limited to below primary level, his literacy is confined to basic Assamese script for everyday tasks. He can comprehend spoken Hindi to a modest extent, especially religious and community contexts, but cannot read or write it fluently. Exposure to English is minimal and largely restricted to occasional workplace terminology.
skills_and_expertise: Jaten is proficient in institutional cleaning and sanitation, adept at floor polishing, laundry handling, and managing cleaning inventories. He possesses practical knowledge of basic maintenance tasks such as minor plumbing and electrical repairs, ensures safety compliance, and coordinates effectively with team members to meet scheduled cleaning routines.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Institutional cleaning","Floor polishing","Laundry handling","Inventory management","Basic maintenance","Time management","Team coordination","Safety compliance","Simple bookkeeping"]
hobbies_and_interests: He enjoys participating in Bihu dances, listening to Assamese folk music, watching cricket matches, gardening, cooking traditional Assamese dishes, volunteering in community events, strolling through tea gardens, and reading devotional literature.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Bihu dance","Assamese folk music","Cricket watching","Gardening","Cooking Assamese dishes","Community volunteering","Walking in tea gardens","Reading devotional literature"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: He aims to progress to a senior housekeeping supervisor role within his institution, mentor junior staff, improve his literacy through adult education classes, and eventually establish a small independent cleaning service after retirement to provide financial security for his family.

He speaks Assamese as his first language and has limited Hindi comprehension. His lifestyle includes late-night chai and a desire to start a cleaning business after retirement.

Write one complete Automated SMS Notifications for clinical domain Psychiatry / Behavioral Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Assamese (code=as, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Psychiatry / Behavioral Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

LENGTH HARD LIMIT: entire SMS ≤ ~500 characters, but you MUST still include every mandatory entity tag below at least once (compact tags OK).

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 49
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: House Keeper (Institutional)
- education_level: Below Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Assam
- district: Dibrugarh
- first_language: Assamese
- clinical_persona_summary: A 49-year-old male from rural Dibrugarh, Assam, currently married, works as an institutional housekeeper with below primary education. He speaks Assamese as his first language and has limited Hindi comprehension. His lifestyle includes late-night chai and a desire to start a cleaning business after retirement.

Document format guide for Automated SMS Notifications (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
CRITICAL: keep the WHOLE SMS under ~400 characters / 2–4 sentences. Not a form dump.
Example:
[[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]: appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]] at [[HOSPITAL_NAME|PHC Kanpur]] on 21-May 10:30 with [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Sharma]]. Confirm on [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]. MRN [[MRN|MRN-2024-001]].

MANDATORY inline entities (6 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

professional_persona: Anoara Das, a 27‑year‑old village‑based home caterer‑to‑be, blends their knack for meticulous budgeting, authentic Bengali sweets preparation, and natural flair for community event coordination into a practical, socially‑driven small‑business vision.
linguistic_persona: Anoara Das, fluent in Bengali, can read and write simple household notes, understands conversational Hindi, and recognizes everyday English words on signs and ads, making them a functional multilingual communicator for village life.
sports_persona: Anoara Das, a passionate weekend kabaddi enthusiast who trains with the local village team and fervently supports the Kolkata Knight Riders during IPL, combines regular matches with a disciplined yoga practice, showcasing their love for teamwork, physical fitness, and community spirit.
arts_persona: Anoara Das, a devoted admirer of Baul folk music who often listens to Paban Das Baul while stitching Kantha quilts and delights in Rabindranath Tagore’s lyrical songs, brings cultural storytelling into everyday conversation, illustrating their deep‑rooted artistic sensibility.
travel_persona: Anoara Das, who primarily explores nearby Kolkata and the Sundarbans during weekend getaways, occasionally saves for a pilgrimage to Varanasi, and prefers planning trips through word‑of‑mouth recommendations from fellow villagers, reflecting their modest yet community‑driven travel style.
culinary_persona: Anoara Das, a skilled home cook who excels at preparing rosogolla, sandesh, mishti doi, and fragrant panta bhat with mustard greens, enjoys sourcing fresh cumin and jaggery from the local market, and treats each family gathering as a culinary showcase, yet sometimes indulges in extra sweets, revealing their sweet‑tooth quirk.
persona: Anoara Das, a sociable, methodical 27‑year‑old from a farming village, balances their love for traditional sweets, community storytelling, and pragmatic financial planning with a curious yet practical mindset.
cultural_background: Born and raised in the agrarian heartland of Barddhaman, West Bengal, Anoara Das grew up immersed in the rich tapestry of Bengali culture. Her daily life follows the rhythms of monsoon‑dependent farming, the colourful celebrations of Durga Puja and Poila Boishakh, and the warm, community‑centric spirit of rural villages where neighbours gather for adda and share homemade sweets like rosogolla. Hindu traditions interweave with folk music, storytelling and a strong sense of hospitality. While she embraces modern ideas, she remains rooted in cultural values of respect for elders, collective harmony, and the simplicity of village life.
linguistic_background: Anoara’s first language is Bengali, and she is a native speaker with functional literacy acquired through primary schooling. She can read and write simple Bengali texts, such as household notes, recipes, and basic letters. Although her formal education did not include a second language, she understands spoken Hindi from regional exposure and can follow basic Hindi conversations. She has limited proficiency in English, primarily recognizing common words on signage and advertisements.
skills_and_expertise: Anoara’s skill set revolves around efficient household management and community engagement. She excels at preparing authentic Bengali dishes and sweets, budgeting family expenses, and coordinating festive events such as Durga Puja pandals. Her strong interpersonal abilities make her a natural facilitator in village “adda” gatherings, where she often mediates conversations and encourages cooperation. Additionally, she possesses basic literacy in Bengali, practical gardening knowledge, and rudimentary digital skills for using a mobile phone to stay connected with relatives and access information.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Bengali cooking', 'Household budgeting', 'Festive event planning', 'Community coordination', 'Interpersonal communication', 'Basic Bengali literacy', 'Gardening', 'Kantha embroidery', 'Mobile phone digital literacy']
hobbies_and_interests: Anoara enjoys lively village ‘adda’ sessions where she engages in storytelling, debates, and sharing of recipes. She is an avid listener of Baul folk music and often practices simple Kantha stitching as a creative outlet. Gardening in her backyard, preparing traditional sweets, and reading devotional tales are regular pastimes. On weekends, she likes to watch local folk dance performances and participate in community sports like kabaddi with friends.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Participating in village adda', 'Listening to Baul music', 'Gardening', 'Cooking Bengali sweets', 'Kantha stitching', 'Reading devotional stories', 'Watching folk dance performances', 'Playing Kabaddi with friends']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Anoara aspires to transform her passion for Bengali cuisine into a modest home‑based catering business, specializing in traditional sweets for festivals and family occasions. She also aims to take a leadership role in her village’s women’s self‑help group, organizing financial literacy workshops and promoting small‑scale entrepreneurship among fellow homemakers. To support these goals, she plans to enrol in an adult literacy program to improve her reading and writing skills and to obtain a short‑term certificate in nutrition and food safety.
persona_clinical_summary: A 27-year-old female from West Bengal, currently married, with a primary education and living in a rural area. She is a homemaker and native Bengali speaker, likely managing household finances and traditional food preparation. Her lifestyle includes community-oriented activities and a focus on family-centric routines.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Bengali (code=bn, script=Bengali). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 27
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: West Bengal
- district: Barddhaman
- first_language: Bengali
- clinical_persona_summary: A 27-year-old female from West Bengal, currently married, with a primary education and living in a rural area. She is a homemaker and native Bengali speaker, likely managing household finances and traditional food preparation. Her lifestyle includes community-oriented activities and a focus on family-centric routines.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---

রোগী: ডাক্তার, আমি রিনা মন্ডল। আমার বয়স 27 বছর এবং আমি গর্ভবতী। আমার 5 মাস চলছে। আমার পেটের নিচের দিকে কিছু ব্যথা হচ্ছে। এটি একটি ভোঁতা ব্যথা, তীব্র নয়। এটি আসে এবং যায়। মাঝে মাঝে আমার মাথাও ঘোরে।
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: নমস্কার রিনা, যোগাযোগ করার জন্য ধন্যবাদ। আমি বুঝতে পারছি আপনি 5 মাসের গর্ভবতী এবং পেটের নিচের দিকে ব্যথা ও মাথা ঘোরার সমস্যায় ভুগছেন। আপনি কি ব্যথা সম্পর্কে আরও কিছু বলতে পারেন? এটি কি সব সময় থাকে নাকি আসে এবং যায়?
রোগী: এটি আসে এবং যায়। বেশিরভাগ সময় সন্ধ্যায়। এটি খুব তীব্র নয়, তবে অস্বস্তিকর। আমি যখন বসা অবস্থা থেকে খুব দ্রুত উঠি, তখন মাথা ঘোরে।
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: আমি বুঝতে পারছি। জরায়ু বড় হওয়ার সাথে সাথে কিছু অস্বস্তি হওয়া স্বাভাবিক। তবে আমাদের সতর্ক থাকতে হবে। আপনার যোনিপথে রক্তপাত বা তরল নিঃসরণ হচ্ছে কি না তা পরীক্ষা করুন। এছাড়াও, সময়ের সাথে সাথে ব্যথা কি বাড়ছে?
রোগী: না, কোনো রক্তপাত নেই। কোনো তরল নেই। ব্যথা একই রকম আছে। এটি কেবল একটি ভোঁতা ব্যথা। আমি মাথা ঘোরা নিয়ে চিন্তিত।
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: মাথা ঘোরা কম রক্তচাপ বা কম রক্তে শর্করার কারণে হতে পারে। অনুগ্রহ করে নিশ্চিত করুন যে আপনি পর্যাপ্ত জল পান করছেন এবং অল্প অল্প করে ঘন ঘন খাবার খাচ্ছেন। খুব দ্রুত উঠে দাঁড়াবেন না। যখন মাথা ঘুরবে, তখন বাম দিকে শুয়ে পড়ুন।
রোগী: ঠিক আছে, আমি চেষ্টা করব। ব্যথার ব্যাপারে কী হবে? আমার কি কোনো ওষুধ খাওয়া উচিত?
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: আপাতত, ব্যথা খুব বেশি হলে আপনি প্যারাসিটামল খেতে পারেন, তবে কোনো ওষুধ খাওয়ার আগে আপনার স্থানীয় আশা কর্মী বা হাসপাতালের সাথে পরামর্শ করুন। সবচেয়ে গুরুত্বপূর্ণ বিষয় হলো আপনার উপসর্গগুলো পর্যবেক্ষণ করা। যদি ব্যথা তীব্র, সব সময় থাকে বা আপনার জ্বর হয়, তবে আপনাকে অবিলম্বে হাসপাতালে আসতে হবে।
রোগী: ঠিক আছে, আমি সতর্ক থাকব। আমার পরবর্তী চেক-আপ কখন হবে?
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: আপনার পরবর্তী রুটিন চেক-আপ আগামী মাসের জন্য নির্ধারিত আছে। তবে আপনার উপসর্গগুলো বিবেচনা করে, আমি চাই আপনি কালনার সাব-ডিভিশনাল হাসপাতালে দ্রুত পর্যালোচনার জন্য আসুন। অনুগ্রহ করে সময় নিশ্চিত করতে হাসপাতালের রিসেপশনে ফোন করুন।
রোগী: আমি আগামীকাল তাদের ফোন করব। ধন্যবাদ ডাক্তার।
ডাক্তার [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: আপনাকে স্বাগতম রিনা। অনুগ্রহ করে নিজের এবং আপনার বাচ্চার যত্ন নিন। আপনার আরও কোনো প্রশ্ন থাকলে, আপনি পোর্টালের মাধ্যমে আমাকে মেসেজ করতে পারেন।

--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Rina Mondal]] [[AGE|27]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-WB-2024-1123]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.wbhealth.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|rina.mondal.wb@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Sub-Divisional Hospital, Kalna]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Doctor, I am Rina Mondal. I am 27 years old and I am pregnant. I am 5 months along. I am having some pain in my lower abdomen. It is a dull ache, not sharp. It comes and goes. I am also feeling a bit dizzy sometimes.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: Hello Rina, thank you for reaching out. I understand you are 5 months pregnant and experiencing some lower abdominal pain and dizziness. Can you tell me more about the pain? Is it constant or does it come and go?
Patient: It comes and goes. Mostly in the evening. It is not very strong, but it is uncomfortable. The dizziness happens when I stand up too quickly from sitting.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: I see. It is common to have some discomfort as the uterus grows. However, we need to be cautious. Please check if you have any vaginal bleeding or leaking fluid. Also, is the pain getting worse over time?
Patient: No, no bleeding. No fluid. The pain is the same. It is just a dull ache. I am worried about the dizziness.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: The dizziness can be due to low blood pressure or low blood sugar. Please make sure you are drinking enough water and eating small, frequent meals. Avoid standing up too quickly. When you feel dizzy, lie down on your left side.
Patient: Okay, I will try that. What about the pain? Should I take any medicine?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: For now, you can take paracetamol if the pain is bothersome, but please check with your local ASHA worker or the hospital before taking any medication. The most important thing is to monitor your symptoms. If the pain becomes sharp, constant, or if you develop a fever, you must come to the hospital immediately.
Patient: Okay, I will be careful. When should I come for a check-up?
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: Your next routine check-up is scheduled for next month. But given your symptoms, I want you to come to the Sub-Divisional Hospital in Kalna for a quick review. Please call the hospital reception to confirm a time.
Patient: I will call them tomorrow. Thank you, Doctor.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Ananya Chatterjee]]: You are welcome, Rina. Please take care of yourself and your baby. If you have any more questions, you can message me through the portal.
judge_reasoning: Bengali prose throughout chat; all TYPEs allowed; female 27 maternal fit; no script/dialect/domain/length issues.

persona: Raghveer Singh blends disciplined retail expertise with a love for Bhangra beats, a habit of organizing every shelf like a ledger, and an unflappable calm that makes them the village’s trusted problem‑solver.
cultural_background: Raghveer Singh belongs to a Sikh family settled in the rural outskirts of Jalandhar, Punjab. Though his mother tongue is Dogri, a language primarily spoken in the Jammu region, his family has lived in the fertile plains of Punjab for generations, absorbing Punjabi customs while preserving their Dogri heritage. The village community is tightly knit, with daily life revolving around the local Gurdwara, agricultural cycles, and seasonal fairs such as Baisakhi. Growing up, he participated in traditional folk music, communal langar preparations, and seasonal harvest festivals, which reinforced a sense of collective responsibility and humility. His cultural identity blends the disciplined ethos of Sikhism, the practical mindset of a farming hinterland, and the curiosity nurtured by a multilingual environment.

His formal education up to higher secondary gave him solid literacy in Hindi; he can comfortably read newspapers, fill forms and maintain ledgers in Hindi. Punjabi, being the lingua franca of Jalandhar, is his third language; he uses it daily at the shop for customer interactions and can understand spoken Punjabi fluently, while his reading ability in Gurmukhi is functional for signage and basic labels. He has basic conversational English, sufficient to understand retail signage and occasional digital payment prompts.
skills_and_expertise: Raghveer's core expertise lies in retail operations. He excels at systematic inventory control, ensuring stock levels are accurately tracked using simple ledger methods. His proficiency in cash handling and point‑of‑sale transactions minimizes errors. Multilingual communication—fluent in Dogri, Hindi, and conversational Punjabi—allows him to serve a diverse clientele with ease. He is skilled at merchandising, arranging product displays in a tidy, visually appealing manner. His disciplined work ethic translates into consistent store hygiene and punctuality. He also possesses basic bookkeeping, using Excel spreadsheets for monthly expense summaries, and has a curiosity for digital payment systems, enabling him to adopt new technologies such as UPI and QR‑code payments with minimal disruption. His calm demeanor helps resolve customer issues calmly and efficiently.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

user_prompt:
Write one complete Discharge Summary for clinical domain General Medicine.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Dogri (code=doi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match General Medicine — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 34
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Shop Attendant
- education_level: Higher Secondary/Intermediate Pre-University/Senior Secondary
- zone: Rural
- state: Punjab
- district: Jalandhar
- first_language: Dogri
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Raghveer Singh, a 34-year-old male, is a shop attendant in rural Jalandhar, Punjab, currently married. He holds a higher secondary education and is fluent in Dogri and Hindi, with English proficiency limited. His lifestyle includes a disciplined routine, regular physical activity, and a diet rich in traditional Punjabi cuisine.

Document format guide for Discharge Summary (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] DOB [[DOB|1994-06-21]] [[AGE|30]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]] | Course / advice: <concise paragraphs matching domain + persona sex/age>

MANDATORY inline entities (14 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- ABHA_ADDRESS — ABHA Address e.g. [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
- CASTE — Caste e.g. [[CASTE|OBC]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

डिस्चार्ज समरी — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Jalandhar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Raghveer Singh]] जन्म तारीख [[DOB|1990-05-15]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Male]]
भर्ती [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-004]] वार्ड [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] बेड [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
डाक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Malhotra]] | कोर्स / सलाह: मरीज़ गी तीव्र पायलोनेफ्राइटिस दे निदान कन्नै भर्ती कीता गेआ हा। उ’नें गी बुखार, कंबणी, ते सज्जे पासे दी कोख च पीर ही। जांच च पेशाब दी रूटीन ते माइक्रोस्कोपी च काफी पायुरिया ते बैक्टीरियोरिया दिक्खेआ गेआ। ब्लड कल्चर च ई. कोलाई (E. coli) पाया गेआ, जेह्‌ड़ा सिप्रोफ्लोक्सासिन लेई संवेदनशील हा। मरीज़ गी इंट्रावेनस सिप्रोफ्लोक्सासिन ते फ्लूइड्स शुरू कीते गे, जेह्‌ड़े क्लिनिकल सुधार पर ओरल सिप्रोफ्लोक्सासिन च बदली दित्ते गे। पिछले 48 घंटें थमां ओह्‌ अफीब्राइल (बुखार मुक्त) ऐ ते उ’दी कोख दी पीर खत्म होई गेई ऐ। मरीज़ गी सलाह दित्ती जंदी ऐ जे ओह्‌ ओरल एंटीबायोटिक्स दा पूरा 14-दिनें दा कोर्स पूरा करे, पर्याप्त हाइड्रेशन बनाए रक्खे, ते इक हफ्ते च अपने प्राइमरी केयर फिजिशियन कन्नै फॉलो-अप करे। उ’दी पत्नी, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]], गी दवाई दी अनुपालन ते बीमारी दे दोबारा होने दे लक्षणें दे बारे च दस्सेआ गेआ। मरीज़ दा [[ABHA_ADDRESS|raghveer.singh@abdm]] सिस्टम च अपडेट करी दित्ता गेआ ऐ। ओह्‌ [[CASTE|SC]] समुदाय कन्नै सरबंध रखदा ऐ ते [[VILLAGE|Banga]] दे ग्रां च रोंदा ऐ। फॉलो-अप लेई उ’दा [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] दर्ज कीता गेआ ऐ। ओह्‌ [[DISTRICT|Jalandhar]] दा निवासी ऐ।

Discharge Summary — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Civil Hospital Jalandhar]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Raghveer Singh]] DOB [[DOB|1990-05-15]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Male]]
Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0815-004]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|A1]] Bed [[BED_NUMBER|05]]
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Vikram Malhotra]] | Course / advice: The patient was admitted with a diagnosis of acute pyelonephritis. He presented with fever, chills, and right-sided flank pain. Investigations including urine routine and microscopy showed significant pyuria and bacteriuria. Blood cultures were positive for E. coli, sensitive to ciprofloxacin. The patient was started on intravenous ciprofloxacin and fluids, which was transitioned to oral ciprofloxacin upon clinical improvement. He has been afebrile for the past 48 hours and his flank pain has resolved. The patient is advised to complete the full 14-day course of oral antibiotics, maintain adequate hydration, and follow up with his primary care physician in one week. His wife, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Jaswinder Kaur]], was counseled on the importance of medication adherence and signs of recurrence. The patient's [[ABHA_ADDRESS|raghveer.singh@abdm]] has been updated in the system. He belongs to the [[CASTE|SC]] community and resides in the village of [[VILLAGE|Banga]]. His [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] is recorded for follow-up. He is a resident of [[DISTRICT|Jalandhar]].
judge_reasoning: Devanagari prose uses appropriate regional dialectal forms matching Dogri expectations; all TYPEs from allow-list; medical content, persona (male 34, pyelonephritis), and entities fully consistent and plausible.

professional_persona: Shobha Toravi is a 45‑year‑old tailor who blends traditional motifs with contemporary cuts, using their vivid imagination to design bespoke wedding and festival wear while juggling day‑to‑day shop management, yet they often wrestle with deadlines because they prefer spontaneous creativity over strict schedules.
linguistic_persona: Shobha Toravi grew up mastering English through daily market chatter and Hindi for wider client communication, now they converse fluently in both, read simple labels and market signs, but their reading and writing remain modest due to limited formal schooling.
sports_persona: Shobha Toravi enjoys early morning walks along the Mangalore shoreline, plays casual badminton at the local community centre, and avidly follows Royal Challengers Bangalore cricket matches, using sport as a brief escape from the tailoring studio.
arts_persona: Shobha Toravi draws inspiration from classical Hindustani music, especially listening to Pandit Jasraj and Zakir Hussain while sketching, admires the vibrant paintings of M.F. Husain, frequently attends Yakshagana performances featuring legendary dancer P.K. Nagappa, and enjoys classic Indian cinema starring Amitabh Bachchan and Savithri, letting these artistic experiences enrich their design aesthetic.
travel_persona: Shobha Toravi prefers short, well‑planned getaways to nearby hill stations like Coorg and coastal retreats such as Goa, but occasionally indulges in spontaneous weekend trips to the Kerala backwaters, balancing limited savings with a yearning for a future Europe adventure.
culinary_persona: Shobha Toravi relishes spicy Mangalorean fare—neer dosa with fish curry, coconut rice, and sweet Mysore pak—preferring to savor them at local eateries, and occasionally experiments with fresh basil and curry leaves in simple home‑cooked dishes, though they rarely cook elaborate meals.
persona: Shobha Toravi is a creatively restless tailor who dreams big, worries often, and juggles artistry with the daily grind of a modest shop.
cultural_background: Shobha Toravi is a Hindu woman from the urban centre of Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, a coastal region known for its blend of Kannada, Tulu and Konkani cultures. Although her family primarily follows the customs of mainland Karnataka—celebrating Ugadi, Deepavali, and local temple festivals—her day‑to‑day life is also shaped by the cosmopolitan atmosphere of Mangalore, where Hindi and English are common lingua‑francas for trade. Growing up in a modest household with limited formal schooling, she absorbed cultural knowledge through community gatherings, family rituals, and the visual richness of regional textiles and artisanal crafts. Her imaginative nature draws her to the colourful masquerade of local festivals and the abstract motifs that appear in coastal folk art.
linguistic_background: Shobha's first language is English; she learned it informally through daily interactions with customers and neighbours. Despite having only below‑primary formal schooling, she can converse fluently, understand basic written English, and uses it to read garment labels, pattern guides and mobile messages. Her second language is Hindi, which she speaks comfortably to a wider Indian clientele and reads simple Hindi scripts such as market signs and devotional pamphlets. She has limited reading and writing proficiency in both languages due to her schooling level, but compensates with strong visual and practical skills.
skills_and_expertise: Shobha is skilled in tailoring and dressmaking, with a keen eye for intricate embroidery and fabric selection. She can draft simple patterns, take precise measurements, and execute custom fittings. Her work includes client consultation, colour coordination, and adapting traditional motifs into contemporary designs. She also manages the day‑to‑day operations of her modest tailoring business, handling budgeting, supplies procurement, and improvisational problem‑solving (jugaad) when faced with material constraints.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Tailoring', 'Dressmaking', 'Embroidery', 'Fabric Selection', 'Pattern Drafting', 'Custom Fitting', 'Client Consultation', 'Small Business Management', 'Creative Problem Solving (Jugaad)']
hobbies_and_interests: Shobha enjoys sketching abstract designs, experimenting with new fabric patterns, and listening to classical Hindustani music while she works. She often attends local craft fairs and folk dance performances for inspiration. In her leisure time, she likes taking walks along the Mangalore beach, gardening with native herbs, reading devotional stories, and watching classic Indian cinema. Occasionally she explores simple DIY home décor projects that combine her love for creativity and practicality.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Sketching', 'Experimenting with Fabric Patterns', 'Listening to Classical Hindustani Music', 'Attending Local Craft Fairs', 'Watching Folk Dance Performances', 'Beach Walks', 'Gardening with Native Herbs', 'Reading Devotional Stories', 'Watching Classic Indian Cinema', 'DIY Home Décor Projects']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Shobha aims to grow her modest tailoring unit into a recognized boutique that specializes in bespoke wedding and festival wear, blending contemporary aesthetics with traditional motifs. She plans to master advanced embroidery techniques, source eco‑friendly fabrics, and mentor a junior apprentice to ensure continuity. Building a strong reputation for artistic excellence and achieving financial stability are key goals. Additionally, she seeks to improve her organisational habits and stress‑management strategies to sustain the business long‑term.
persona_clinical_summary: A 45-year-old female tailor from Dakshina Kannada, Karnataka, currently married, with below-primary education and English as her first language. She works in an urban tailoring shop, blending traditional and contemporary designs while managing daily operations. Clinically, she reports frequent worry and creative restlessness, with limited formal schooling but functional English communication skills.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

professional_persona: Rababen Koli is a budding home‑based entrepreneur focusing on Gujarati culinary crafts, blending meticulous kitchen management with creative folk‑art flair, while their curiosity and organized nature keep innovations grounded in practical jugaad.
linguistic_persona: Rababen Koli fluently speaks Gujarati, reads and writes simple household notes and recipes in Gujarati, converses in Hindi using basic phrases learned from television, and understands common English words on product labels.
sports_persona: Rababen Koli follows cricket passionately, cheering for the Gujarat Titans in the IPL and the Saurashtra team in domestic matches, regularly joins the local women's badminton club for evening games, and occasionally enjoys back‑yard kho‑kho with neighbours during festivals.
arts_persona: Rababen Koli delights in Gujarati folk songs by Hemant Chauhan and Kinjal Dave, sways to Bollywood classics featuring Madhuri Dixit, creates vibrant rangoli designs inspired by traditional motifs, and practices sketching scenes of village life with a humble set of coloured pencils.
travel_persona: Rababen Koli prefers short trips within Gujarat, often visiting the white salt desert of the Rann of Kutch during the full‑moon festival, the historic Somnath temple, and the bustling markets of Ahmedabad for craft supplies, while dreaming of a weekend getaway to Goa's beaches when the family budget allows.
culinary_persona: Rababen Koli loves cooking traditional Gujarati fare, especially dhokla, khandvi, undhiyu, and her tangy homemade mango pickle, seasoning with hing, mustard seeds and fresh coriander, and she savors the crunch of jalebi and dhokla as snack treats.
persona: Rababen Koli is a creatively curious homemaker who blends meticulous household management with folk‑art flair, juggling budget worries and a steady stream of community duties.
cultural_background: Rababen belongs to the Koli community of rural Bhavnagar in Gujarat. The Kolis are traditionally linked to fishing and farming and are celebrated for their vibrant folk traditions, especially Garba and Dandiya dance during Navratri and communal gatherings at the local mandir. Village life follows seasonal agricultural cycles, festivals, and close‑knit family networks. The community values hospitality, collective harmony, and practical ingenuity (jugaad) in daily chores. Growing up surrounded by Gujarati customs, regional cuisine, and oral storytelling of Koli folk songs, Rababen’s curiosity drives her to explore new folk art motifs and experiment with local craft techniques, while she keeps a disciplined routine for household responsibilities.
linguistic_background: Rababen's mother tongue is Gujarati, and she is fully literate in the Gujarati script, having completed primary schooling. She can read and write basic Gujarati texts, especially domestic notes, recipes, and simple letters. Her formal education did not include structured Hindi or English instruction, but she has picked up conversational Hindi from television and community interactions and can understand simple Hindi prayers. Her English exposure is limited to occasional use of English words in daily conversation (e.g., lakhs, crore) and basic reading of labels, but she does not read or write English fluently.
skills_and_expertise: Rababen is proficient in managing a rural household, including meal planning, preparing traditional Gujarati dishes such as dhokla, thali, and farsan, and maintaining kitchen hygiene. She has practical skills in sewing, mirror work embroidery, and creating simple handicrafts like bamboo baskets and decorative diyas. Her organizational ability helps her keep household accounts, budgeting for festivals, and coordinating community events like village fairs and Navratri gatherings. She also possesses basic knowledge of herbal remedies used in the household and can assist neighbours with modest medical advice. Her curiosity about art has led her to experiment with folk painting motifs (Rangoli designs) and basic sketching.
skills_and_expertise_list: ["Household management", "Traditional Gujarati cooking", "Sewing and mirror work embroidery", "Bamboo basket weaving", "Basic herbal remedy knowledge", "Community event coordination", "Budgeting and financial planning", "Folk painting (Rangoli designs)", "Basic sketching"]
hobbies_and_interests: Rababen enjoys listening to Gujarati folk music and participating in Garba during Navratri. She loves experimenting with new recipes and decorating dishes with creative garnishes. In her leisure, she paints simple rangoli patterns, practices basic sketching, and weaves bamboo items. Gardening, especially growing seasonal vegetables for her kitchen, provides her with calm. She also follows local news, reads devotional books in Gujarati, and occasionally watches Hindi films to unwind. Her curiosity leads her to explore online videos about craft techniques whenever she finds time.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Listening to Gujarati folk music", "Participating in Garba and Dandiya", "Experimenting with new recipes", "Creating rangoli designs", "Basic sketching", "Bamboo weaving", "Gardening seasonal vegetables", "Reading devotional books", "Watching Hindi films", "Exploring online craft tutorials"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Rababen wishes to deepen her expertise in Gujarati culinary arts and traditional crafts, with the ambition to launch a modest home‑based business selling homemade pickles, farsan, and embroidered items. She aims to enrol in government‑run skill development programs for rural women to learn advanced embroidery and small‑scale food processing. Long‑term, she hopes to become financially independent enough to support her parents, fund her younger siblings' education, and contribute to community development projects. While she values her role as a homemaker, she also aspires to nurture her curiosity about art by attending local workshops and possibly mentoring other village girls.

She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, and her first language is Gujarati. Her lifestyle includes managing household duties and community responsibilities, with a diet rich in traditional Gujarati cuisine.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

user_prompt:
Write one complete Referral Letter for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Gujarati (code=gu, script=Gujarati). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 28
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: No Occupation / Retired / Homemaker
- education_level: Primary
- zone: Rural
- state: Gujarat
- district: Bhavnagar
- first_language: Gujarati
- clinical_persona_summary: Rababen Koli is a 28-year-old female from rural Bhavnagar, Gujarat, who is never married and has completed primary education. She is a homemaker with no formal occupation, and her first language is Gujarati. Her lifestyle includes managing household duties and community responsibilities, with a diet rich in traditional Gujarati cuisine.

Document format guide for Referral Letter (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-01]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]], [[AGE|36]] / [[GENDER|Male]], District [[DISTRICT|...]]
Reason: <domain-appropriate clinical reason matching persona>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- REFERRAL_ID — Referral ID e.g. [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-1123]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PASSPORT_NUMBER — Passport Number e.g. [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|Z1234567]]
- DRIVING_LICENCE — Driving Licence Number e.g. [[DRIVING_LICENCE|UP14 20110012345]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
રેફરલ [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Vallabhipur]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]] તરફથી
વિષે: [[PATIENT_NAME|Rababen Koli]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Female]], જિલ્લા [[DISTRICT|Bhavnagar]]
કારણ: ગંભીર એનિમિયા અને ગર્ભાવસ્થા દરમિયાન હાઈપરટેન્શન સાથે હાઈ-રિસ્ક ગર્ભાવસ્થા.

પ્રિય સાહેબ/મેડમ,

આ અમારા દર્દી, રાબાબે કોલી, જે 28 વર્ષની ગૃહિણી છે અને વલ્લભીપુર ગામની રહેવાસી છે, તેને વિશેષ પ્રસૂતિ સંભાળ માટે રેફર કરવા માટે છે. તે 28 અઠવાડિયાની ગર્ભાવસ્થામાં પ્રથમ વખત માતા બનવાની છે અને તેને ગંભીર આયર્ન ડેફિસિયન્સી એનિમિયા (Hb: 6.8 g/dL) નો ઇતિહાસ છે અને નવી નિદાન થયેલ ગર્ભાવસ્થા દરમિયાન હાઈપરટેન્શન (BP: 150/100 mmHg) છે. તેના છેલ્લા પ્રસૂતિ પૂર્વેના ચેક-અપમાં હળવા પ્રોટીન્યુરિયા જોવા મળ્યા હતા. તેને ક્યારેક માથાનો દુખાવો અને પગના નીચેના ભાગમાં સોજાની ફરિયાદ છે.

તપાસ: તેના કમ્પ્લીટ બ્લડ કાઉન્ટમાં માઇક્રોસાઇટિક હાયપોક્રોમિક એનિમિયા જોવા મળે છે. યુરિન રૂટિનમાં 2+ પ્રોટીન જોવા મળે છે. પેટનું અલ્ટ્રાસાઉન્ડ એક જીવંત ગર્ભને સેફાલિક પ્રેઝન્ટેશનમાં સામાન્ય એમ્નિયોટિક ફ્લુઇડ ઇન્ડેક્સ સાથે દર્શાવે છે.

કૃપા કરીને તેની ગર્ભાવસ્થાને હાઈ-રિસ્ક કેસ તરીકે મેનેજ કરો, જેમાં નજીકથી દેખરેખ અને સંભવિત વહેલી ડિલિવરીની જરૂરિયાતને ધ્યાનમાં લો. તેના [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] તમારા સંદર્ભ માટે જોડાયેલ છે. તેનો સંપર્ક [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]] પર કરી શકાય છે. તેના પિતા, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Karsanbhai Koli]], તેની સાથે આવશે. તેના [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|G8765432]] અને [[DRIVING_LICENCE|GJ0320200123456]] ઓળખ માટે ઉપલબ્ધ છે.

આભાર.

ડૉ. રાજેશ પટેલ
MBBS, MS (પ્રસૂતિ અને સ્ત્રી રોગશાસ્ત્ર)
પ્રાથમિક આરોગ્ય કેન્દ્ર, વલ્લભીપુર
[[HOSPITAL_ID|PHC-VAL-001]]
generated_text_en:
Referral [[REFERRAL_ID|REF-2024-0815]] from [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Primary Health Centre, Vallabhipur]] / [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Patel]]
Re: [[PATIENT_NAME|Rababen Koli]], [[AGE|28]] / [[GENDER|Female]], District [[DISTRICT|Bhavnagar]]
Reason: High-risk pregnancy with severe anemia and gestational hypertension.

Dear Sir/Madam,

This is to refer our patient, Rababen Koli, a 28-year-old homemaker from Vallabhipur village, for specialized obstetric care. She is a primigravida at 28 weeks of gestation with a history of severe iron deficiency anemia (Hb: 6.8 g/dL) and newly diagnosed gestational hypertension (BP: 150/100 mmHg). Her last antenatal check-up revealed mild proteinuria. She reports occasional headaches and swelling in her lower extremities.

Investigations: Her complete blood count shows microcytic hypochromic anemia. Urine routine shows 2+ protein. Ultrasound abdomen shows a single live fetus in cephalic presentation with normal amniotic fluid index.

Please manage her pregnancy as a high-risk case, considering the need for close monitoring and potential early delivery. Her [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] is attached for your reference. She can be contacted at [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]. Her father, [[RELATIVE_NAME|Karsanbhai Koli]], will accompany her. Her [[PASSPORT_NUMBER|G8765432]] and [[DRIVING_LICENCE|GJ0320200123456]] are available for identification.

Thank you.

Dr. Rajesh Patel
MBBS, MS (Obstetrics & Gynaecology)
Primary Health Centre, Vallabhipur
[[HOSPITAL_ID|PHC-VAL-001]]

user_prompt:
Write one complete Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript for clinical domain Maternal Health.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Hindi (code=hi, script=Devanagari). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.
DOMAIN ANCHOR: chief complaint, findings, and plan MUST match Maternal Health — do not default to unrelated TB/generic content unless that is the assigned domain.

PLACEMENT (device/network): Put IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, and MAC_ADDRESS exactly once each in a short session/metadata header. Do NOT repeat them in chat turns. Chat turns use patient/doctor/phone/email/appointment only.
Also put AGE and GENDER exactly once in the session header (e.g. Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]). Do NOT re-tag AGE/GENDER/PHONE/EMAIL/HOSPITAL_NAME in every turn.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Female
- age: 23
- marital_status: Never Married
- occupation: Juice Clarification Supervisor
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Jharkhand
- district: Sahibganj
- first_language: Hindi
- clinical_persona_summary: A 23-year-old female juice clarification supervisor from rural Jharkhand, never married, with an illiterate education level. She speaks Hindi orally, recognizes numerals and safety signs, and works in a production floor setting. Her lifestyle includes folk music, yoga, and occasional mango consumption.

Document format guide for Telemedicine / Doctor-Patient Chat Transcript (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton — put demographics + device/network IDs ONLY in a short
session header (once each), then keep chat turns free of ID/metadata spam:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|34]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|...]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|98XXXXXXXX]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|...]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]]
--- chat ---
Patient: <symptom turn — no AGE/GENDER/PHONE re-tags>
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]: <advice turn>

MANDATORY inline entities (12 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- EMAIL_ADDRESS — Email Address e.g. [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|meena.devi@example.com]]
- APPOINTMENT_ID — Appointment ID e.g. [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
- IP_ADDRESS — IP Address e.g. [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]]
- URL — URL (Personal Website/Profile) e.g. [[URL|https://example.org/u/meena]]
- IMEI_NUMBER — IMEI Number e.g. [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
- MAC_ADDRESS — MAC Address e.g. [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
--- सत्र ---
रोगी [[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Kumari]] [[AGE|23]] [[GENDER|Female]]
अपॉइंटमेंट [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-02]] पोर्टल [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
क्लाइंट IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] डिवाइस IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] फोन [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
ईमेल [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|sita.kumari@example.com]] अस्पताल [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Sahibganj]]
--- चैट ---
रोगी: नमस्ते डॉक्टर। मैं सीता कुमारी हूँ। मेरी उम्र 23 साल है। पिछले दो दिनों से मुझे बहुत कमजोरी और चक्कर आ रहे हैं। मेरे पेट के निचले हिस्से में भी दर्द हो रहा है।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: नमस्ते सीता। मैं डॉक्टर प्रिया सिंह हूँ। मैं आपकी बात सुन रही हूँ। मुझे दर्द के बारे में और बताएं। क्या यह तेज़ दर्द है या हल्का?
रोगी: यह हल्का दर्द है, जैसे लगातार दर्द हो रहा हो। यह ज्यादातर मेरे पेट के निचले हिस्से में होता है। मुझे उल्टी आने का भी मन करता है।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: मैं समझ गई। क्या आपने अपनी योनि से कोई रक्तस्राव देखा है?
रोगी: नहीं डॉक्टर, कोई रक्तस्राव नहीं है। बस दर्द और कमजोरी है। मैं चिंतित हूँ।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: यह अच्छी बात है कि कोई रक्तस्राव नहीं है। यह आपके गर्भावस्था से संबंधित हो सकता है। आप कितनी सप्ताह की गर्भवती हैं?
रोगी: मुझे लगता है कि मैं लगभग 16 सप्ताह की हूँ। मेरा आखिरी मासिक धर्म 15 मई को था।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: ठीक है, सीता। आपके हल्के दर्द और चक्कर आने के लक्षणों के आधार पर, हमें सावधान रहने की आवश्यकता है। मैं चाहती हूँ कि आप तुरंत सदर अस्पताल साहिबगंज जाएं और अल्ट्रासाउंड करवाएं। कृपया खुद गाड़ी न चलाएं।
रोगी: ठीक है डॉक्टर। लेकिन मैं कैसे जाऊँगी? मैं एक गाँव में रहती हूँ।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: मैं समझती हूँ। क्या आप अपने पति या किसी रिश्तेदार को बुला सकती हैं जो आपको ले जा सके? यदि नहीं, तो मैं एम्बुलेंस की व्यवस्था कर सकती हूँ। कृपया मुझे बताएं।
रोगी: मेरी माँ आ सकती हैं। वह एक घंटे में यहाँ होंगी।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: यह अच्छा है। कृपया अस्पताल जाएं और स्कैन करवाएं। रिपोर्ट से पता चलेगा कि सब कुछ ठीक है या नहीं। स्कैन से पहले भारी भोजन या पेय न करें।
रोगी: ठीक है डॉक्टर। धन्यवाद। मैं अब जाती हूँ।
डॉक्टर [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: आपका स्वागत है, सीता। कृपया मुझे अपडेट रखें। अपना ख्याल रखें।
generated_text_en:
--- session ---
Patient [[PATIENT_NAME|Sita Kumari]] [[AGE|23]] [[GENDER|Female]]
Appt [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240815-02]] Portal [[URL|https://tele.example.in/visit]]
Client IP [[IP_ADDRESS|103.21.244.12]] Device IMEI [[IMEI_NUMBER|356938035643809]]
MAC [[MAC_ADDRESS|00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E]] Phone [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
Email [[EMAIL_ADDRESS|sita.kumari@example.com]] Hospital [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Sahibganj]]
--- chat ---
Patient: Namaste doctor. I am Sita Kumari. I am 23 years old. I am feeling very weak and dizzy for the last two days. My lower abdomen is also paining.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Hello Sita. I am Dr. Priya Singh. I am listening to you. Tell me more about the pain. Is it sharp or dull?
Patient: It is a dull pain, like a constant ache. It is mostly in my lower belly. I also feel like I might vomit.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: I see. Have you noticed any bleeding from your vagina?
Patient: No doctor, no bleeding. Just the pain and weakness. I am worried.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: It is good that there is no bleeding. This could be related to your pregnancy. How many weeks pregnant are you?
Patient: I think I am about 16 weeks. My last period was on 15th May.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: Okay, Sita. Based on your symptoms of dull pain and dizziness, we need to be cautious. I want you to go to the District Hospital Sahibganj immediately for an ultrasound. Please do not drive yourself.
Patient: Okay doctor. But how will I go? I live in a village.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: I understand. Can you call your husband or a relative to take you? If not, I can arrange for an ambulance. Please tell me.
Patient: My mother can come. She will be here in an hour.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: That is good. Please go to the hospital and get the scan done. The report will tell us if everything is okay. Do not eat or drink anything heavy before the scan.
Patient: Okay doctor. Thank you. I will go now.
Dr [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Priya Singh]]: You are welcome, Sita. Please keep me updated. Take care.
judge_reasoning: Hindi Devanagari prose matches expected language; all TYPEs in allow-list; female 23yo maternal persona fits domain; Latin tags and names expected, no shifts or violations.

professional_persona: Gangavva Mathapati is a seasoned vehicle and interior cleaner who combines meticulous dust‑removal techniques with traditional cleaning agents like soda ash and neem leaves, and they pragmatically experiment with simple new tools to boost efficiency while consistently delivering courteous service to clients.
linguistic_persona: Gangavva Mathapati speaks fluent spoken Kannada, skillfully using local idioms and gestures to convey instructions, and they pick up occasional Hindi words from neighbours while understanding basic work‑related English terms like 'vehicle' and 'cleaner'.
sports_persona: Gangavva Mathapati enjoys cheering for the Royal Challengers Bangalore during IPL matches, follows the Indian cricket team's games, and occasionally participates in community badminton sessions at the local Ramanagara club, staying modestly active while preferring low‑impact activities.
arts_persona: Gangavva Mathapati immerses themselves in Kannada folk music, especially songs by K.S. Natha and Hamsalekha, regularly watches classic Dr. Rajkumar films at the Ramanagara community hall, and they enjoy crafting simple beaded decorations inspired by local folk art.
travel_persona: Gangavva Mathapati prefers short, well‑planned trips to nearby destinations such as Nandi Hills, Mysore Palace, and the Tirumala shrine, balancing modest budgets with the desire to experience cultural festivals and family gatherings, and they often travel by shared auto or personal car with a small group of relatives.
culinary_persona: Gangavva Mathapati loves preparing simple traditional dishes such as ragi mudde and bisi bele bath, relishes the earthy flavor of ragi flour, tamarind, and fresh coriander, and they prefer home‑cooked meals over restaurant food, often using seasonal vegetables from their garden.
persona: Gangavva Mathapati is a diligent yet modest cleaning entrepreneur who mixes age‑old neem‑based tricks with occasional gadget cravings, manages money conservatively while secretly splurging on shiny tools, and quietly battles knee arthritis with daily turmeric tea.
cultural_background: Gangavva Mathapati belongs to the Kannada‑speaking community of Ramanagara, a town on the outskirts of Bengaluru known for its granite hills and silk‑cocoon markets. Growing up in an urban environment, she absorbed the everyday rhythms of South‑Indian life – the early‑morning temple bells, the aroma of fresh ragi mudde, and the vibrant celebrations of Ugadi, Dasara and Deepavali. Her family embraces traditional joint‑family values, where respect for elders and a sense of collective duty are paramount. This cultural backdrop shapes her practical outlook, balancing a curiosity for new cleaning tools (jugaad) with a deep reverence for time‑tested household practices.
linguistic_background: Gangavva's linguistic repertoire centres on spoken Kannada. Being illiterate, she cannot read or write Kannada or any other script, but she comfortably engages in daily conversations, understands local idioms, and follows oral instructions at work. She has no formal knowledge of a second language, though occasional Hindi phrases from neighbours seep into her speech. Her exposure to Indian English is limited to work‑related terms such as 'vehicle', 'window' and 'cleaner' that she picks up through listening.
skills_and_expertise: Gangavva is skilled in thorough vehicle interior cleaning, adept at removing dust, stains and odours from seats, carpets and dashboards. She efficiently cleans windows of cars, homes and commercial premises, using a mix of traditional cleaning agents like soda ash, neem leaves and modern supplies where available. Her expertise includes surface disinfection, careful handling of cleaning tools, time management for multiple jobs in a day, and courteous interaction with clients, even though she communicates mainly through gestures and simple spoken Kannada.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Vehicle interior cleaning', 'Window cleaning', 'Surface disinfection', 'Use of traditional cleaning agents', 'Time management', 'Client interaction']
hobbies_and_interests: In her leisure time, Gangavva enjoys listening to Kannada folk songs on the radio, visiting the local temple during festivals, and tending to a small garden of seasonal vegetables and herbs. She loves preparing simple traditional dishes such as ragi mudde and bisi bele bath for family gatherings. Occasionally she engages in hand‑craft activities like beading or making simple decorative items, and she likes watching regional movies at the community hall.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to Kannada folk songs', 'Temple visits during festivals', 'Gardening seasonal vegetables and herbs', 'Cooking traditional Kannada dishes', 'Hand‑crafting simple decorative items', 'Watching regional movies']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Gangavva aims to secure a steady flow of cleaning contracts in Ramanagara, hoping to become the trusted go‑to cleaner for local households and small businesses. She wishes to upgrade her tools with a modest motorised vacuum cleaner and to pick up a few efficient cleaning shortcuts (jugaad) from experienced peers. Over the next few years she plans to mentor younger women in her community, helping them earn a livelihood through similar work, while also building a comfortable financial safety net for her family’s future.
occupation: Vehicle, Window and Related Cleaners, Other
persona_clinical_summary: A 55-year-old female from Karnataka, Ramanagara, who is currently married, works as a vehicle cleaner, has no formal education, and speaks only Kannada. She lives in an urban area and manages her health conservatively while dealing with knee arthritis. Her lifestyle includes using traditional cleaning methods and occasional gadget purchases.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

Format examples (copy the bracket pattern; invent new synthetic values):

  [[ABHA_ADDRESS|meena.devi@abdm]]
  [[ABHA_ID|12-3456-7890-1234]]
  [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
  [[AGE|34]]
  [[APPOINTMENT_ID|APT-240521-01]]
  [[ASHA_WORKER_NAME|Sunita Yadav]]
  [[BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER|50200012345678]]
  [[BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER|SBIN0001234]]

  [[BPL_RATION_CARD|BPL-UP-208001-4421]]
